questions.q: A filtration $(\mathcal{F}_n)_{n\ge 0}$ on $(\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})$ is required to satisfy:
questions.hint: A filtration is an increasing chain of sub-$\sigma$-algebras: information accumulates over time.
questions.explain: By definition the filtration is increasing: $\mathcal{F}_n\subseteq\mathcal{F}_{n+1}$, and each $\mathcal{F}_n$ is a sub-$\sigma$-algebra of $\mathcal{F}$. Information accumulates; nothing is forgotten.

questions.q: Let $(X_n)$ be a sequence of random variables and $\mathcal{F}_n=\sigma(X_0,\ldots,X_n)$ the natural filtration. Which random time is a stopping time?
choices: $\tau=$ the time at which $\sum X_n$ achieves its all-time maximum
questions.hint: A stopping time $\tau$ requires $\{\tau\le n\}\in\mathcal{F}_n$ — you can decide at time $n$ whether stopping has happened, using only the information available so far.
questions.explain: $\{\tau\le n\}=\{\exists k\le n: X_k\ge 5\}\in\mathcal{F}_n$, so the first hitting time is a stopping time. The other three involve looking into the future (last visit, all-time max), which $\mathcal{F}_n$ cannot decide.

questions.q: Symmetric simple random walk $S_n$ on $\mathbb{Z}$ starts at $S_0=0$. Let $\tau=\min\{n\ge 0: S_n\in\{-2,3\}\}$. What is $\mathbb{P}(S_\tau=3)$?
questions.hint: Gambler's ruin on $\{-2,\ldots,3\}$: the probability of hitting $b=3$ before $a=-2$ starting at $0$ is $(0-(-2))/(3-(-2))=2/5$.
questions.explain: Gambler's ruin formula for symmetric walk on $\{-2,\ldots,3\}$: $\mathbb{P}(\text{hit }3\text{ first})=(0-(-2))/(3-(-2))=2/5=0.4$. The fact that $\tau$ is a stopping time is what lets the formula apply.

questions.q: An adapted, integrable process $(M_n)$ is a martingale when:
questions.hint: The martingale identity is the conditional one: given everything up to time $n$, the best predictor of $M_{n+1}$ is $M_n$.
questions.explain: The defining property is $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n$. Constant mean and independent increments are consequences (or stronger conditions) but not equivalent. Monotonicity contradicts the fair-game intuition.

questions.q: Let $(X_i)$ be i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{P}(X_i=+1)=\mathbb{P}(X_i=-1)=1/2$ and $S_n=X_1+\cdots+X_n$. Which of the following is also a martingale w.r.t. $\mathcal{F}_n=\sigma(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{E}[S_{n+1}^2\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=S_n^2+\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}^2]+2S_n\mathbb{E}[X_{n+1}]=S_n^2+1$, so $S_n^2-n$ subtracts off the predictable drift to give a martingale. $S_n^2$ is a submartingale; $|S_n|$ likewise; $nS_n$ has drift $S_n$.

questions.q: A Doob martingale is the process
choices: $M_n=$ the running maximum of an i.i.d.\ sequence
questions.hint: The Doob martingale of a random variable $Z$ is its sequence of conditional expectations along an increasing filtration; the tower property gives the martingale identity automatically.
questions.explain: By the tower property $\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Z\mid\mathcal{F}_{n+1}]\mid\mathcal{F}_n]=\mathbb{E}[Z\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$, so $M_n=\mathbb{E}[Z\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$ is a martingale. Sums and products of i.i.d.\ variables can be martingales too, but those are not the Doob construction.

questions.q: Doob's optional-stopping theorem $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=\mathbb{E}[M_0]$ holds when $M$ is a martingale and $\tau$ a stopping time provided
choices: at least one of: $\tau$ is bounded, $M$ is uniformly integrable, or $\tau<\infty$ a.s. and $(M_{n\wedge\tau})$ is bounded
questions.hint: Three classical sufficient hypotheses: bounded $\tau$, uniform integrability of $M$, or a.s.-finite $\tau$ with $(M_{n\wedge\tau})$ uniformly bounded.
questions.explain: All three are sufficient; the symmetric random walk stopped at $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n=1\}$ has $\tau<\infty$ a.s. but $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=1\ne 0$, showing none of the hypotheses can be dropped.

questions.q: Symmetric random walk $S_0=0$, $\tau=\inf\{n:S_n\in\{-3,5\}\}$. Use optional stopping on $S_n$ to compute $\mathbb{P}(S_\tau=5)$.
questions.hint: $\tau$ is bounded a.s.\ and $(S_{n\wedge\tau})$ is bounded by $\max(|-3|,5)=5$, so optional stopping gives $0=\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=5p+(-3)(1-p)$.
questions.explain: Optional stopping (case (c)): $0=\mathbb{E}[S_0]=\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=5p-3(1-p)=8p-3$, so $p=3/8=0.375$. The same trick with $S_n^2-n$ gives $\mathbb{E}[\tau]=15$.

questions.q: Wald's identity says: for i.i.d.\ integrable $X_i$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu$ and a stopping time $\tau$ with $\mathbb{E}[\tau]<\infty$,
questions.hint: Apply optional stopping to the centred martingale $S_n-n\mu$: under $\mathbb{E}\tau<\infty$ one gets $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau-\mu\tau]=0$.
questions.explain: The martingale $M_n=S_n-n\mu$ has $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau]=0$ by optional stopping (under $\mathbb{E}\tau<\infty$ and appropriate integrability), giving $\mathbb{E}[S_\tau]=\mu\,\mathbb{E}[\tau]$. This is Wald's identity.

questions.q: The Doob decomposition writes an adapted $L^1$ process $X_n$ uniquely as
choices: $X_n=M_n\cdot A_n$ with $M$ a martingale and $A$ a stopping time
questions.hint: Doob: every adapted $L^1$ process splits uniquely as a martingale plus a predictable compensator (with $A_0=0$).
questions.explain: The compensator is $A_n=\sum_{k=1}^n\mathbb{E}[X_k-X_{k-1}\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]$ (predictable with $A_0=0$); subtracting it gives the martingale part. Sub/super-martingales are exactly those for which $A$ is non-decreasing/non-increasing.

questions.q: Symmetric simple random walk $S_n$, $X_n=S_n^2$. The predictable compensator is $A_n=n$. What is $\mathbb{E}[A_{10}]$?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{E}[X_k-X_{k-1}\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]=\mathbb{E}[2S_{k-1}X_k+X_k^2\mid\mathcal{F}_{k-1}]=0+1=1$, so $A_n=n$. In particular $\mathbb{E}[A_{10}]=10$, and $S_n^2-n$ is the martingale part.

questions.q: For a square-integrable martingale $M$ with $M_0=0$, the predictable quadratic variation $\langle M\rangle_n$ is
questions.hint: By definition $\langle M\rangle$ is the unique predictable increasing process making $M_n^2-\langle M\rangle_n$ a martingale.
questions.explain: $\langle M\rangle_n$ is the Doob compensator of the submartingale $M_n^2$: predictable, increasing, $\langle M\rangle_0=0$, and $M_n^2-\langle M\rangle_n$ is a martingale. The pathwise sum $[M]_n=\sum(\Delta M_k)^2$ is the (different) optional quadratic variation.

questions.q: Doob's martingale convergence theorem says: if $(M_n)$ is a martingale with $\sup_n\mathbb{E}|M_n|<\infty$, then
choices: $M_n\to M_\infty$ almost surely for some $M_\infty\in L^1$
questions.hint: $L^1$-boundedness gives a.s. convergence to an $L^1$ limit; $L^1$-convergence requires the stronger uniform-integrability hypothesis.
questions.explain: $L^1$-boundedness implies a.s. convergence (Doob's upcrossing inequality) to an $L^1$ random variable $M_\infty$. Without UI, $L^1$-convergence can fail and the limit need not equal $M_0$ in expectation.

questions.q: Let $X_i$ be i.i.d.\ with $\mathbb{P}(X_i=2)=\mathbb{P}(X_i=0)=1/2$, and $M_n=\prod_{i=1}^n X_i$ (product martingale). Which is true?
choices: $M_n$ converges in $L^1$ to a random variable with mean $1$
questions.hint: $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=1$ so $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$ by independence. But $M_n=0$ as soon as any $X_i=0$, which happens a.s.\ in finitely many steps.
questions.explain: $M_n$ is a non-negative martingale with $\mathbb{E}[M_n]=1$, but $M_n=0$ once any $X_i$ takes the value $0$, which happens a.s. So $M_n\to 0$ almost surely. The convergence is not in $L^1$ — this is the canonical $L^1$-bounded-but-not-UI example.

questions.q: For the $L^1$-convergence $M_n\to M_\infty$ in $L^1$ to hold, the additional hypothesis required is
questions.hint: UI is exactly what closes the gap between $L^1$-bounded and $L^1$-convergent: $M_n\to M_\infty$ in $L^1$ iff $(M_n)$ is UI.
questions.explain: Uniform integrability is the precise condition: a martingale converges in $L^1$ iff it is uniformly integrable, in which case $M_n=\mathbb{E}[M_\infty\mid\mathcal{F}_n]$. $L^2$-boundedness is sufficient but stronger than necessary.

choices: independent stationary increments with $W_t-W_s\sim\mathcal{N}(0,t-s)$ and continuous paths a.s.
questions.hint: $W_0=0$, increments are independent and $\mathcal{N}(0,t-s)$-distributed for $0\le s<t$, and paths are continuous but nowhere differentiable.
questions.explain: Independent stationary Gaussian increments $W_t-W_s\sim\mathcal{N}(0,t-s)$ and continuous (but nowhere differentiable, infinite total variation) paths characterise Brownian motion.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a martingale w.r.t.\ the natural filtration of Brownian motion?
questions.hint: $W_t^2$ is a submartingale; subtracting the predictable $t$ gives the martingale $W_t^2-t$.
questions.explain: $\mathbb{E}[W_t^2\mid\mathcal{F}_s]=W_s^2+(t-s)>W_s^2$, so $W_t^2$ is a submartingale, not a martingale. The compensator $t$ subtracts off, leaving $W_t^2-t$ as the martingale; the exponential martingale is the engine of Girsanov.

questions.q: For Brownian motion on $[0,T]$, the quadratic variation $[W]_T$ along a refining partition converges (in probability) to what value when $T=2$?
questions.hint: $[W]_t=t$ a.s.\ — quadratic variation along any sequence of partitions with mesh $\to 0$ converges in probability to $t$.
questions.explain: $\sum_k(W_{t_{k+1}}-W_{t_k})^2\to t$ in probability as the mesh shrinks; at $t=T=2$ this equals $2$. This is the signature of Brownian paths: finite quadratic variation, infinite total variation.
